**INTERNAL PROPOSAL**

**ProxyClaude**

**CLI-First Shared Development Platform**

Cost Reduction and Platform Modernisation Proposal for Software Development Teams

| Prepared for | CTO / IT Director / Senior Technical Management |  |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Date | June 2026 |  |
| Version | 1.0 — Initial Proposal |  |
| Status | For Review and Approval |  |

| CURRENT MONTHLY SPEND $1,725 Developer Claude seats | PROPOSED MONTHLY SPEND $1,280 ProxyClaude \+ VPS | ANNUAL SAVING $5,340 26% cost reduction |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |

# **1\. Executive Summary**

This proposal recommends adopting **ProxyClaude**, a CLI-first shared developer platform, as the primary environment for all software development project work. The primary driver is cost: the current per-seat Claude subscription model costs **$1,725 per month** in developer access fees. ProxyClaude reduces that to **$1,280 per month**, saving **$445 every month and $5,340 every year** — a 26 percent cost reduction. Every additional capability described in this document (persistent sessions, Git sync, staging automation, security governance) comes on top of that saving at no incremental subscription cost.

| Bottom line for leadership ProxyClaude is not a cost to justify — it is a cost saving that also delivers a better developer environment. The platform pays for its own infrastructure and returns $5,340 per year to the business. |
| :---- |

## **What changes**

* Developers stop using individual claude.ai Pro and Max seats for their project work.

* All project-based Claude usage moves to a shared ProxyClaude platform running on VPS infrastructure.

* Non-developer roles (editors, marketers, managers) keep their individual seats unchanged.

* Savings begin in Phase 2 of the rollout, approximately five to eight weeks after project start.

## **What stays the same**

* Developers retain full Claude Code capability inside their projects.

* Existing project workflows — local Git sync and direct environment access — continue unchanged.

* Security standards improve: the platform adds centralised audit logs, RBAC, and workspace isolation.

# **2\. Cost Analysis**

## **2.1  Current state: per-seat subscription spend**

The team currently holds two tiers of Claude subscriptions. The breakdown below covers developer-facing seats only. Non-developer seats are excluded from this analysis as they are out of scope for migration.

| Subscription tier | Seats | Unit cost / month | Monthly total |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----: |
| Normal team (claude.ai Pro) | 34 | $25 | $850 |
| Premium users (claude.ai Max) | 10 | $125 | $1,250 |
| **Gross total (44 individual seats)** | **44** |  | **$2,100** |
| Less: 2 premium seats removed (For non-technical user) |  |  | −$250 |
| Less: 5 normal seats removed (For non-technical user) |  |  | −$125 |
| **Net active developer Claude spend** |  |  | **$1,725 / month** |

*Note: the 27 remaining normal seats and 8 remaining premium seats belong to non-developer roles and are not affected by this proposal.*

## **2.2  Proposed state: shared platform spend**

ProxyClaude consolidates all developer Claude access into a shared platform model. The cost structure replaces per-seat subscriptions with two line items: a team plan and VPS infrastructure.

| Component | Quantity | Unit cost / month | Monthly total |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----: |
| ProxyClaude team plan (shared platform access) | 4 teams | $100-$200 | $400-$800 |
| Ubuntu VPS servers (workspace infrastructure) | 4 servers | $120 | $480 |
| **Total proposed monthly spend** |  |  | **$880-$1,280 / month** |

## **2.3  Direct saving**

| Metric | Amount |
| :---- | ----: |
| Current net developer Claude spend | $1,725 / month |
| Proposed spend (ProxyClaude \+ VPS) | $1,280 / month |
| **Monthly saving** | **$445 / month** |
| **Annual saving** | **$5,340 / year** |
| Cost reduction | 26% |
| Payback period (infrastructure build cost) | \< 3 months |

## **2.4  Three-year cumulative projection**

The saving is not a one-time event. Because the platform replaces recurring per-seat fees, the benefit compounds every month.

| Period | Monthly saving | Cumulative saving | Business equivalent |
| :---- | ----: | ----: | :---- |
| Month 6 | $445 | $2,670 | 4 VPS servers covered for 5+ months |
| End of Year 1 | $445 | $5,340 | Covers all VPS costs for \~11 months |
| End of Year 2 | $445 | $10,680 | Part-time developer equivalent |
| **End of Year 3** | **$445** | **$16,020** | Equivalent to \~2 full developer months |

| Why the saving holds over time VPS server costs are fixed infrastructure. Adding a new developer costs zero additional infrastructure. New developers join the platform rather than joining the per-seat subscription. The saving grows proportionally as the team expands. |
| :---- |

# **3\. Why ProxyClaude Costs Less**

The financial saving is structural, not a negotiated discount. It comes from four fundamental differences in how Claude access is priced and consumed.

| Mechanism | Current model | ProxyClaude model |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Pricing unit** | Per seat: each developer pays $25–$125/month regardless of usage level | Per team: one $200/month plan covers all developers on that team |
| **Scaling cost** | Linear: adding one developer adds one seat and one monthly fee | Fixed: adding a developer to the platform costs $0 extra subscription |
| **Infrastructure cost** | None — but also no shared workspace, no central control, no isolation | $120/server/month: fixed regardless of how many developers use it |
| **Premium seat elimination** | Developers using Claude Code for project work need $125/month Max seats | Covered by the shared platform. Those $125/month seats are cancelled |

*The single largest saving comes from eliminating Premium (Max) seats. Each Premium seat costs $125/month. Two developers moving to ProxyClaude saves $250/month on those seats alone — more than half a VPS server's cost.*

# **4\. What the Platform Delivers**

The cost saving is the primary justification for this proposal. The capabilities described below are **additional gains** that come at no extra cost once the platform is in place. They are presented as evidence that the platform is superior to the status quo, not as the primary reason to proceed.

## **4.1  CLI-first developer access**

Developers connect to their VPS workspace from a local terminal using a single command. There is no browser UI dependency at the start of the workflow. The experience is deliberately close to a standard terminal session.

| $ proxyclaude login $ proxyclaude connect client-plugin userA@proxyclaude:\~/projects/client-plugin$ claude |
| :---- |

The developer lands directly inside the correct project folder on the VPS with Claude Code, Git, and the full project toolchain ready to use. No setup. No configuration. Identical environment for every developer on every project.

## **4.2  Persistent sessions**

Normal SSH connections break when a laptop sleeps, a network drops, or a VPN reconnects. If Claude is running inside a raw SSH connection, that interruption stops the work. ProxyClaude manages a persistent session layer on the VPS so that Claude continues running even when the developer's connection is lost. Reconnection restores the exact same terminal state.

| Layer | Problem solved | Approach |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| SSH keepalive | Idle disconnects | ServerAliveInterval, TCPKeepAlive |
| Persistent session | Work lost on disconnect | Session manager backed by tmux or systemd |
| CLI reconnect | Manual re-setup after drop | proxyclaude reconnect attaches to same session |

## **4.3  Two project execution modes**

The platform supports two common execution scenarios that cover the majority of software project workflows, without any additional tooling or subscriptions.

### **Mode 1: Local development via Git bridge**

* Claude edits code inside the VPS project workspace.

* Claude commits and pushes changes to GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.

* A lightweight local sync agent on the developer’s machine pulls the changes.

* The local project folder updates automatically.

* Developer refreshes their local environment and reviews the result.

* **Best for:** any code-first project: libraries, APIs, plugins, themes, custom applications, scripts, or content management customisations.

### **Mode 2: Direct remote environment access**

* Developer provides the remote environment URL, admin credentials, and optionally SSH access.

* **Claude selects the safest available tool:** SSH for server tasks, CLI tools for environment operations, admin dashboards for configuration changes, Playwright/Chromium for visual automation.

* Claude applies changes, clears cache if applicable, and confirms the remote environment is working.

* Developer refreshes the remote environment and reviews the result.

* **Best for:** remote environment updates, admin-panel changes, configuration tasks, visual verification, CMS or platform management.

| Production safety rule Direct remote access mode defaults to staging/test environments only. Access to production environments requires a separate approval workflow, backup confirmation, and explicit admin grant. This is enforced at the platform level, not left to individual developer judgement. |
| :---- |

## **4.4  Central admin control and audit**

The current model gives management no visibility into how individual Claude seats are being used. ProxyClaude provides a central control plane that addresses this directly.

| Capability | Detail |
| :---- | :---- |
| User provisioning (MVP Scope) | Admin creates accounts, assigns workspaces and projects, resets credentials, suspends users |
| Session monitoring | Real-time view of who is connected, what project they are in, session duration |
| Audit logs | All logins, SSH connections, Git pushes, staging access, and admin actions are logged |
| Role-based access control | Developers access only their assigned projects. Admin access is separately scoped. No developer has root or sudo. |
| Project assignment | Each developer is assigned specific projects. Cross-project access requires admin action. |

# **5\. Platform Architecture**

The diagram below shows the complete ProxyClaude CLI-First architecture. It covers all six functional zones: developer entry, authentication and control plane, CLI login and connect flow, VPS runtime, project execution modes, and the optional future web layer.

![Full architecture diagram][image1]

*Figure 1: ProxyClaude CLI-First Architecture — developer-first access via ProxyClaude CLI \+ SSH, Git sync, and staging automation.*

## **5.1  Zone 1 — Developer entry point**

The developer's primary tool is the ProxyClaude CLI installed on their local machine. This is the only software they need to install. They run four key commands from any terminal:

| Command | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- |
| **proxyclaude login** | Authenticate with the backend over HTTPS, receive a JWT token stored in local keychain |
| **proxyclaude projects** | List all projects the developer is assigned to by the admin |
| **proxyclaude connect \<project\>** | Register or reuse an SSH key, then open a persistent SSH session directly into the project folder on the VPS |
| **proxyclaude reconnect** | Re-attach to an existing session on the VPS after a network drop or laptop sleep |

An optional local project environment and local sync agent run on the developer’s machine for Mode 1 (Git bridge) workflows. These are not required for remote-only work.

## **5.2  Zone 2 — Authentication and control plane**

All developer requests pass through a centralised backend before reaching the VPS. This is the control layer that enforces security, tracks sessions, and manages admin operations.

* **Backend API (REST \+ WebSocket):** Node.js service handling auth, project registry, SSH key registration, admin actions, and audit events.

* **Auth Service:** Issues and validates JWT tokens. Manages login sessions and token expiry.

* **PostgreSQL database:** Stores users, roles, projects, SSH key registrations, sessions, and the full audit log.

* **SSH Key Registration:** On first connect, the CLI generates a local SSH key pair and registers the public key with the backend. The backend writes it to the correct VPS user account.

* **Admin Provisioning Service:** Admin creates users, assigns projects, resets credentials, and revokes access through this service via admin CLI or future dashboard.

* **Security layer:** HTTPS/TLS 1.2+, bcrypt/argon2 password hashing, JWT session tokens, RBAC, and audit logging on all actions.

## **5.3  Zone 3 — CLI login and connect flow**

The six-step connect sequence is the core interaction pattern. It happens automatically when a developer runs proxyclaude connect.

| Step | What happens |
| :---: | :---- |
| **1** | Developer runs proxyclaude login. CLI sends credentials to the backend API over HTTPS. |
| **2** | Backend verifies credentials, checks role and project access, returns a JWT token. |
| **3** | CLI generates a local SSH key pair (or reuses an existing one) and sends the public key to the backend. |
| **4** | Backend registers the public key on the correct VPS user account (authorized\_keys). |
| **5** | Developer runs proxyclaude connect \<project\>. CLI retrieves the SSH connection config from the backend. |
| **6** | CLI opens an SSH session directly into the project folder inside a persistent session manager (tmux). The developer is now inside their workspace and runs claude. |

## **5.4  Zone 4 — Runtime on VPS**

The Ubuntu VPS hosts one isolated Linux workspace per developer. Each workspace is a separate Linux user account with a private home directory. Developers cannot access each other’s workspaces.

* **Each workspace contains:** a projects/ folder, Claude Code CLI, Git, project-specific CLIs (WP-CLI, Composer, npm, pip, etc.), Playwright/Chromium, SSH client, and a project config/secrets directory.

* **Persistent session manager:** tmux or a systemd-backed equivalent keeps the terminal alive. Claude continues running even if the SSH connection drops.

* **Current isolation:** Linux user per developer (MVP). Future upgrade: Docker container per user or per project for stronger isolation.

* **Resource controls:** CPU, RAM, and disk quotas enforced per user via cgroups or systemd slices to prevent one developer affecting others.

## **5.5  Zone 5 — Project execution modes**

Once inside their workspace, developers have two workflows available, selected based on whether they are working locally or against a remote environment.

* **Mode 1 (Local development via Git bridge):** Claude edits code on the VPS, commits and pushes to GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket. A local sync agent on the developer’s machine pulls the changes into the local project folder. Best for code-first work across any language or framework.

* **Mode 2 (Direct remote environment access):** Developer provides the remote environment URL, admin credentials, and optional SSH access. Claude selects the safest tool: SSH for server operations, CLI tools for environment management, admin dashboards for configuration, Playwright/Chromium for visual automation. Best for remote updates, admin tasks, and visual verification.

## **5.6  Zone 6 — Future web access layer**

The CLI-first architecture is designed so that a web layer can be added later without changing the backend or VPS workspaces. The web layer uses the same backend API and the same VPS workspaces that the CLI already uses.

* **Web login page:** Username/password login through a browser, producing the same JWT session as the CLI.

* **Browser terminal (xterm.js):** Full terminal rendered in the browser, connecting to the same persistent VPS session the developer uses via CLI.

* **Admin dashboard:** Web UI for user management, project assignment, session monitoring, logs, and metrics.

* **Not required for core CLI workflow:** The web layer is an optional enhancement for teams who prefer browser access. It does not affect the cost saving, which is realised from Phase 2 (CLI access) onwards.

# **6\. Technology Stack**

The platform is built on standard, widely-supported open source technologies. There are no proprietary dependencies and no vendor lock-in beyond the Claude API itself.

| Layer | Technologies | Rationale |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| CLI | Node.js, TypeScript, Oclif | Cross-platform packaging, simple command structure, active ecosystem |
| Backend API | Node.js, NestJS or Express, Prisma | Strong structure for auth, projects, admin APIs, and future web layer |
| Database | PostgreSQL | Reliable relational store for users, roles, projects, sessions, audit logs |
| SSH / runtime | OpenSSH, SSH certificates, tmux, bastion gateway | Secure, persistent developer access. Short-lived SSH certificates over static keys |
| VPS infrastructure | Ubuntu, Nginx or Caddy, systemd or PM2 | Standard Linux platform with reverse proxy and process management |
| Developer toolchain | Claude Code, Git, project CLIs (WP-CLI, Composer, npm, pip, etc.) | Full project toolchain pre-installed and customisable per workspace |
| Browser automation | Playwright and Chromium | Reliable browser control for visual tasks, admin actions, and verification |

# **7\. Implementation Roadmap — 3-Week MVP \+ Phased Build**

The MVP is deliverable in **35 hours total**. It covers the minimum viable scope to get developers connecting via CLI, Claude running inside project folders, and individual seat cancellations underway. The hourly estimates below are conservative and include buffer for testing and iteration.

| MVP target: end of 35 hours By end of 35 Hours, a developer can run proxyclaude login, proxyclaude connect \<project\>, and claude inside a real project folder on the VPS. That is sufficient to begin cancelling individual Claude seats and generating the $445/month saving. The 35-hour investment is recovered within the first month of operation. |
| :---- |

## **7.1  MVP hourly summary**

| Week | Focus | Hours | Key milestone |
| :---- | :---- | ----: | :---- |
| 1 | Backend API, auth, SSH key registration, admin provisioning, VPS setup | 11 | Backend live, admin can create users |
| 2 | ProxyClaude CLI, SSH connect flow, workspace setup, Claude Code installed | 12 | Developer connects via CLI, Claude runs in project |
| 3 | Persistent sessions (tmux), reconnect, Git bridge, local sync agent, MVP validation | 12 | MVP complete, seat cancellations begin |
| **TOTAL** | Full MVP: backend \+ CLI \+ sessions \+ Git bridge | **35** | $445/month saving begins. Payback \< 1 month. |

## **7.2  Post-MVP phases**

After the MVP is live and seat cancellations have begun, the following phases extend the platform incrementally. Each phase is independent — the saving continues regardless of which post-MVP phases are built.

| Phase | Timing | Est. hrs | Scope | Outcome |
| :---- | :---- | ----: | :---- | :---- |
| 4 | Weeks 4–5 | 10–12 | Remote environment credential model, SSH helpers, cache clearing, environment verification | Direct remote access available |
| 5 | Weeks 6–7 | 8–10 | Playwright/Chromium, admin dashboard scripts, visual verification, screenshots | Visual and admin tasks supported |
| 6 | Weeks 8–10 | 16–20 | Web login, browser terminal (xterm.js), admin dashboard with sessions, logs, metrics | Browser access added on top of CLI core |
| **7** | **Weeks 11+** | 20+ | Docker per user/project, quotas, observability, backups, incident runbooks | Production hardening complete |

| Cost saving timeline The saving begins at end of Week 3 when MVP is verified and seat cancellations start. At $445/month, the 35-hour build investment is recovered within the first month of operation. Post-MVP phases are optional enhancements that add capability without adding subscription cost. |
| :---- |

# **8\. Security and Governance**

Moving to a shared platform does not increase security risk — it reduces it. The current model distributes Claude access across unmanaged individual accounts with no audit trail and no central revocation. ProxyClaude centralises all of this.

## **7.1  Access controls**

* HTTPS/TLS for all API and web traffic.

* Passwords hashed with bcrypt or argon2. No plaintext storage.

* Role-based access control: admin and developer roles are strictly separated.

* Short-lived SSH certificates preferred over static SSH keys for production.

* Central SSH bastion/gateway as the single controlled entry point.

* No root or sudo permissions for developer workspace users.

## **7.2  Workspace isolation**

* Each developer maps to an isolated Linux user with a private home directory.

* Users cannot access other users’ project folders or platform secrets.


## **7.3  Audit and compliance**

* All logins, SSH certificate issuance, project access, session events, Git pushes, staging access, and admin actions are logged.

* Logs are stored centrally and are accessible to admin users via dashboard or CLI.

* Staging access defaults to staging-only. Production access requires explicit approval.

* Git branching is enforced: Claude commits to feature branches. PRs are required to merge.

# **9\. Risks and Mitigation**

The risks below are manageable with standard engineering and operational controls. None are blockers.

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
| :---- | :---: | :---- |
| SSH session drops interrupt Claude tasks | **Medium** | Persistent session manager (tmux/systemd). Work continues after disconnect. proxyclaude reconnect restores state. |
| Shared VPS resource contention | **Medium** | Per-user CPU/RAM/disk quotas via cgroups. Max sessions per user. Monitoring. Docker upgrade path in Phase 9\. |
| Credential or secret leakage | **High** | Secrets encrypted at rest. Short-lived SSH certificates. Least-privilege project scopes. Full audit logging of all credential access. |
| Git conflicts with local project environment | **Low** | Diff prompt before auto-pull. Local backup before sync. Conflict detection. Developer confirmation required. |
| Playwright/browser automation fragility | **Low** | SSH and environment CLIs are primary tools. Playwright is a fallback. Retry logic built in. Human review mandatory for visual tasks. |
| Database / binary assets not in Git | **Low** | Git handles code only. Database/asset snapshots handled per-project via appropriate CLI tools. Scope clearly communicated to developers. |
| Security misconfiguration during setup | **Medium** | No sudo for workspace users. Permission checks enforced in code. Bastion gateway as single entry. Automated security tests in CI. |

# **10\. Recommendation and Next Steps**

## **10.1  Recommendation**

This proposal recommends approval to build ProxyClaude as the shared development platform for all software project work. The financial case stands on its own: $445 saved per month, $5,340 per year, with a payback period under three months. Everything else in this document — the CLI workflow, persistent sessions, Git bridge, staging automation, central audit controls — represents additional operational value at no incremental cost.

| This is not a cost to justify ProxyClaude pays for its own infrastructure from the first month of full operation and continues returning budget to the organisation every month thereafter. Delaying adoption is a choice to continue paying $445/month more than necessary. |
| :---- |

## **10.2  Requested approvals**

1. **Approve Phase 1 build.**  Authorise development of the backend API, authentication, RBAC, and admin provisioning. This is the foundation on which all subsequent phases depend.

2. **Approve Phase 2 build.**  Authorise the ProxyClaude CLI, SSH key/certificate integration, and connect command. This is the phase that enables seat cancellations and begins generating the monthly saving.

3. **Agree seat cancellation schedule.**  Confirm which individual Claude seats will be cancelled once Phase 2 is verified in production. This converts the projected saving into an actual one.

4. **Schedule 90-day review.**  Set a review point 90 days after Phase 2 goes live to validate the saving, confirm developer adoption, and authorise Phases 3 onwards.

## **10.3  Success criteria**

The proposal will be considered successful when all of the following are confirmed:

* Developer can run proxyclaude login and proxyclaude connect \<project\> from any local terminal.

* Developer lands directly inside the correct VPS project folder with Claude Code available.

* Network disconnection does not interrupt an active Claude session.

* Claude can safely commit and push project changes through Git.

* Local mode can pull changed project files automatically into the local environment.

* Remote access mode can perform SSH and CLI operations on a remote/staging environment.

* Admin can create, suspend, and audit users and their project access.

* Monthly Claude subscription spend is reduced by at least $445 from the baseline of $1,725.

ProxyClaude Production Architecture | Internal Proposal | June 2026
